what_it_does: A first-person recount of a manual model-fleet run over ~5,052 files of the ElevenLabs Wizard corpus, describing what was done, what broke, how thinking changed, the capture design, engineering realities, and remaining open questions.
observations: Run took place around 2026-06-04 to 2026-06-05, with the report written around 2026-06-09.
The corpus is treated as scattered, unreviewed AI echoes of Tim's mental model, not reliable surface artifacts.
Local LLM was vLLM serving cyankiwi/Qwen3.5-4B-AWQ-4bit at 32K context on a WSL2 box with 16GB VRAM.
Embeddings used bge-m3 at 1024 dimensions; cloud reasoner was Kimi via Ollama with 256K context.
A degenerate repetition loop in the 4B caused ~20% failures, fixed by a repetition_penalty ladder rather than by raising token budgets.
Calibration raised success from 79% to 98% on a sample, but the repetition penalty may suppress legitimate enumerative content.
Capture is framed as multi-level projections (structural, content, meaning, epistemic, generative) rather than a single extraction.
Forms route files into light versus substance buckets; about 35% of files were status-or-log bookkeeping.
Only Stage 1 (legibility map) was built and calibrated; Stages 2-5 remain designed but unrun.
The DB currently holds a stratified 99-file sample, not the full corpus.
Enforced JSON schema output was required; json_object alone was unreliable.
VRAM limits forced killing one server to run the other; co-loading 4B and bge-m3 was unreliable.
The author explicitly notes being behind current app state and asks readers to defer to their own knowledge there.
standouts: The degenerate-repetition-loop diagnosis and the discipline of reading raw output before theorizing.
The reframe from single-file extraction to multi-level projections.
The cascade economics: local and human reasoning are free, cloud is finite, Tim's reasoning is scarce.
The unresolved tradeoff that repetition_penalty may censor legitimate repeated content.
conventions: First-person, self-aware voice with explicit dates and acknowledged obsolescence.
Absolute paths and registry-driven configuration in ~/wizard-run-1/registries/.
Resume-safe, single-writer persistence with per-record commits.
Versioned prompts and schema-enforced JSON output.
Render-not-judge framing for per-file capture; judgment reserved for later aggregate passes.
Explicit separation of forms (routing) and projections (meaning lenses).
concerns: Author self-describes the report as out of date relative to current app state.
Full corpus run was prepared but not executed; 98% success is sample-only.
Forms and projections coexist in a slightly redundant, untidy way.
Several important issues are explicitly unresolved, especially repetition_penalty vs legitimate repetition.
No code symbols are present; this is pure narrative/documentation.
notes: A self-aware, first-person after-action report of a manual corpus-reconstruction run, heavy on model-concurrency and embedding lessons, with Stage 1 calibrated but Stages 2-5 unbuilt.
questions: Who is the exact author of this report, and what is their current role?
What is the precise current shape of the Company engine and MCP tooling being built?
What does the ElevenLabs Wizard project look like now, given the report is several days old?
What are the exact contents of the projection and prompt registries?
How will the unresolved repetition_penalty tradeoff be validated in practice?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is a first-person recount and transfer of lessons from one run; the file matches that purpose while also acting as a cautionary guide for tool builders.
apparent_themes: Corpus as lossy echoes of intent
Cascade economics and scarce human reasoning
Epistemic humility and reading raw output
intention_signals: Pass ground-level operational knowledge from a manual prototype to the people building engine nodes and MCP tools, with special emphasis on diagnosing failures by looking at actual output rather than theorizing.
novelty: Unusually candid first-person account that foregrounds dead-ends, reframes, and unresolved tradeoffs; also proposes a multi-level projection embedding design and an inversion-finder for cross-level relations.
contribution: Keeps the capture design (forms, projections, marks), the repetition-penalty ladder with its caveat, the cascade economics, the read-raw-output debugging reflex, registry-driven prompts, resume-safe single-writer persistence, and the multi-vector-per-item embedding strategy.
summary_for_embedding: First-person field report documenting a manual model-fleet run over ~5,000 files of the ElevenLabs Wizard corpus, including capture design, degenerate-loop diagnosis, cascade economics, and open engineering questions.
intention_for_embedding: Transfer ground-level lessons from a by-hand corpus-reconstruction prototype to builders of concurrency, embedding, and MCP tooling.
